description: Cross-paper edges in the Perfectoid Spaces / Scholze program DAG. 8 papers, 2011–2021. Spans the lineage Scholze→{BMS1,BMS2}→Prismatic, the Kedlaya–Liu parallel development, and the geometric arc Diamonds→Fargues–Scholze.

edges.note: Perfectoid correspondence at the field level. KL via Witt vectors + primitive ideals; Scholze via tilting $K^\flat = \varprojlim K$. Independent simultaneous proofs (Scholze 2011-11, KL 2013-01).
Galois group equivalence $G_K \cong G_{K^\flat}$ — independent proofs.
Tilting at the algebra level. Different engines: KL uniform-Banach + primitive-ideal; Scholze almost-math + cotangent vanishing.
Same goal, structurally different machinery (Witt-vector vs. mod-$\varpi$ deformation).
Affinoid Spa homeomorphism across tilting.
KL: stably-uniform implies sheafy. Scholze: almost-Tate acyclicity.
Finite-étale tensor equivalence across tilting.
Almost purity via different routes.
Equivalence of perfectoid spaces with their characteristic-$p$ counterparts.
Étale local systems / sites equivalence across tilting.
Recovers Fontaine's $(\varphi, \Gamma)$-modules inside the relative perfectoid framework.
Relative Fargues–Fontaine vector-bundle dictionary built on perfectoid framework.
Both exploit 'char-$p$ side controls char-$0$ side' to lift filtrations.
Scholze–Weinstein imports Scholze's inverse-limit-perfectoid criterion (Perfectoid Spaces Thm 7.17) to license $\mathcal{M}_\infty$ as a perfectoid space.
Infinite-level Rapoport–Zink space as a preperfectoid adic space — first major application of perfectoid theory to Shimura-style moduli.
Hodge-Tate classification of $p$-divisible groups: a perfectoid-side classification using the tilting framework.
BMS1 imports Scholze's earlier rational $p$-adic Hodge comparison (downstream of perfectoid technology) to bootstrap to integral level.
$A\Omega$ master comparison extends tilting / perfectoid covers to integrally-functorial cohomology theory.

Both papers build coherent-cohomology theories living over $A_{\mathrm{inf}}$-like rings: BMS1's $R\Gamma_{A_{\mathrm{inf}}}$ and KL's $\varphi$-modules over relative Robba — independent realizations of the 'integral / Witt-vector' upgrade.
BMS2 identifies $\widehat{\mathbbl{\Delta}}_A \simeq A\Omega_A$, recasting BMS1's $A\Omega$ via topological Hochschild homology. Same cohomology object, new construction.
$\pi_0 \mathrm{TC}^-(R; \mathbb{Z}_p) \cong A_{\mathrm{inf}}(R)$ is a statement about perfectoid rings as defined by Scholze — anchors the homotopy-theoretic recasting in the perfectoid foundations.
Motivic filtration on $\mathrm{THH}/\mathrm{TC}^-/\mathrm{TP}$ extends BMS1's $A\Omega$ comparison into a filtered framework with $K$-theoretic interpretation.
Bhatt–Scholze recast Scholze's perfectoid rings as the perfect objects of a new category — perfect prisms. Definition lifted into $\delta$-ring deformation theory.
Tilting equivalence becomes a special case of the perfect-prism dictionary.
Almost purity in the prismatic framework: extends Scholze's almost purity to perfectoidization of integral algebras.
Almost-purity engine difference: prismatic uses André's flatness lemma; Scholze uses cotangent vanishing + tilting.
Étale comparison generalizes Scholze's étale-site tilting to cohomological-coefficients form.
Master prismatic theorem unifies all $p$-adic cohomology theories; tilting becomes one input.
$A\Omega$ recovered as Frobenius-twisted prismatic cohomology. Subsumes BMS1 inside the prismatic framework.
Discreteness + $p$-torsion-freeness of $\mathbf{Z}_p(n)$ extends BMS2's cohomological identification with logarithmic de Rham–Witt.
Identifies the Nygaard completion with BMS2's $\widehat{\mathbbl{\Delta}}$, re-deriving the BMS2 ↔ BMS1 bridge inside prismatic.
Perfect-prism dictionary subsumes KL's primitive-ideal formulation.
Algebra-level perfectoid correspondence recast as 'perfect $\delta$-rings'.
Almost purity extended from finite-étale to perfectoidization-of-integral case.
Filtered cohomological structures recovering classical $p$-adic Hodge theory.
Diamonds explicitly imports Scholze's almost-purity theorem from Perfectoid Spaces.
Diamonds explicitly imports the étale-site tilting from Perfectoid Spaces.
Diamonds imports Kedlaya–Liu's almost-purity (thm:3.6.21 and thm:5.5.9 cited in text).
Diamond functor $X \mapsto X^\diamondsuit$ extends perfectoid spaces to a wider category (locally spatial diamonds) closed under $v$-stack operations.
Cohomological smoothness in the diamond category is a far-reaching extension of perfectoid étale-site tilting.
Poincaré duality for diamonds is a categorical / six-functor strengthening of the kind of duality first hinted at in Scholze's weight-monodromy application.
$\mathrm{Bun}_G$ as a $v$-stack with cohomological-smoothness statements relies critically on Diamonds' six-functor formalism.
Hecke functor preservation of compactness / ULA uses Diamonds' constructibility theorem.
$\mathrm{Bun}_G$ is a diamond-style $v$-stack — uses the diamond functor to import adic-space data.
Categorical local Langlands runs inside Diamonds' Verdier-duality framework.
Geometric Satake / dual-group construction extends the infinite-level Rapoport–Zink perfectoid story.
$\mathrm{Bun}_G$ ultimately runs on perfectoid covers of the FF curve — perfectoid foundations.
HN-polygon semicontinuity extends Kedlaya–Liu's slope-polygon semicontinuity to the FF-curve / $\mathrm{Bun}_G$ setting.
FF-curve vector-bundle classification extends KL's $\varphi^a$-module / FF-bundle dictionary into the absolute setting.

BMS1's $R\Gamma_{A_{\mathrm{inf}}}$ is built on perfectoid covers whose Banach-algebra structure KL formalized: KL thm:5.3.9 establishes that the relative Robba rings $\tilde{\mathcal{R}}^{[s,r]}_R$ are relatively perfectoid, providing the sheaf-theoretic substrate. BMS1 uses this implicitly when invoking 'sheafy structure presheaves on perfectoid affinoids'.

Both papers organize $\varphi$-module data over $A_{\mathrm{inf}}$-like rings into module categories: BMS1 via Breuil–Kisin–Fargues modules, KL via $\varphi^a$-bundles on the relative FF curve. Independent constructions of equivalent categorical frameworks.

Almost purity in different formulations: BMS1 implicitly uses the almost-zero cohomology of perfectoid covers (the $A\Omega$ master comparison rests on it); KL states almost purity directly. Both proved within ~3 years via different routes.
BMS1's torsion comparison theorem provides the integral-cohomology bounds that prismatic uses to derive the odd $K$-theory vanishing application. The arithmetic chain runs BMS1 → BMS2 → prismatic.

$B_{\mathrm{dR}}^+$-cohomology framework is one of the period-ring scaffolds Fargues–Scholze use when relating $\mathrm{Bun}_G$ to classical $L$-parameter spaces. The Hodge-Tate / de Rham comparison underlies the geometric Satake construction's coefficient theory.

BMS2's $\mathfrak{S}$-cohomology theory extends BMS1's $R\Gamma_{A_{\mathrm{inf}}}$ object: same shape (perfect complex, $\varphi$-linear endomorphism), but with finer base-ring structure and a topological-Hochschild-homology origin.

BMS1's $q$-de Rham computation on the torus is the local model that motivated the systematic motivic filtration of BMS2: the $q$-deformation interpolates between Hodge-Tate ($q=1$) and crystalline (Frobenius-twisted), suggesting a filtered framework with graded pieces.

Both papers exploit fpqc/$v$-style descent for structure sheaves: BMS2 via fpqc-sheaf property of cotangent powers, Diamonds via $v$-topology subcanonicity. Same conceptual mechanism (faithful flatness implies sheaf descent for the relevant cohomology theory) deployed in adjacent contexts.

Both identify $A_{\mathrm{inf}}(R)$ structurally for $R$ perfectoid: BMS2 as $\pi_0 \mathrm{TC}^-(R; \mathbb{Z}_p)$ via topological Hochschild homology; prismatic as the perfect-prism side of the dictionary. Independent realizations of $A_{\mathrm{inf}}$ as a canonical functorial invariant of perfectoid rings.

Both papers use logarithmic de Rham–Witt / FF-curve frameworks to organize cohomology of perfectoid-related objects: BMS2 identifies $\mathbb{Z}_p(n) \simeq W\Omega^n_{X,\log}[-n]$; FS classifies vector bundles on $X_C$ via isocrystals. Common ancestor: the Witt-vector / period-ring machinery.

Cohomological smoothness and étale-coefficients comparison both run on the same six-functor formalism over perfectoid-style $v$-stacks. Prismatic's étale comparison uses the nearby-cycles framework; Diamonds developed the formalism in which 'nearby cycles' is well-defined.

Diamond functor and prismatic master comparison both let cohomology theories be formulated over $p$-adic formal schemes by passing to a perfectoid-style framework. Prismatic uses the diamond setting implicitly when stating étale comparison over the adic generic fibre.

Fargues–Scholze's semiorthogonal decomposition of $D(\mathrm{Bun}_G, \Lambda)$ along the Newton stratification depends critically on Diamonds' six-functor compatibilities — tensor compatibility, projection formulas, and base change for $D^+_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}$ are what make the excision triangles defining the decomposition well-formed.

FS's categorical Langlands correspondence requires base-change invariance under extension of algebraically-closed nonarchimedean base fields — this is exactly Diamonds thm:1.13. Otherwise the categorical functor wouldn't be well-defined across base-field extensions.
$\mathrm{Bun}_G$ being a $v$-stack relies on Diamonds' establishing that the relevant prestacks (perfectoid spaces, étale maps) are $v$-stacks. FS imports this descent foundation directly.

Prismatic étale comparison and FF-curve classification both rest on the same perfectoid foundation: étale cohomology of the adic generic fibre identified via Frobenius fixed points. The vector-bundle classification on $X_C$ in FS is the geometric realization of the perfect-prism dictionary.

Perfect-prism / perfectoid dictionary and FF-curve isocrystal classification are dual organizations of the same arithmetic data: a perfectoid ring's $A_{\mathrm{inf}}$ side carries Frobenius-equivariant structure that, on the Fargues–Fontaine side, becomes a vector bundle with prescribed slopes.

Scholze–Weinstein's infinite-level Rapoport–Zink space as a preperfectoid adic space was one of the earliest examples motivating the diamond formalism — the construction $X \sim \varprojlim X_n$ becomes a representable diamond exactly when the limit is perfectoid. The Diamonds paper formalizes this pattern.

RZ-tower duality (Faltings–Fargues–Scholze–Weinstein) is the structural prototype for the Hecke / dual-group symmetry that FS extends to a geometric Satake-style equivalence on $\mathrm{Bun}_G$. The infinite-level perfectoid moduli were the first place this duality became explicit.

Hodge-Tate classification of $p$-divisible groups (free $\mathbb{Z}_p$-module + $C$-subspace data) provides the local-systems input the FS dual-group construction operates on at the geometric level. The dictionary $\{p\text{-div groups}\} \leftrightarrow \{(T,W)\}$ is the fibrewise content of the geometric Satake equivalence.

Both papers organize the algebraic data of $p$-divisible groups over $\mathcal{O}_C$: SW via the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_\infty$, BMS1 (via Fargues, thm:4.28) via the Breuil–Kisin–Fargues module classification. Sister statements: BMS1 is the algebra side, SW is the geometry side of the same arithmetic phenomenon.

Diamonds' diamond functor extends KL's perfectoid-adic-space framework: KL thm:8.3.5 gives the first systematic perfectoid-vs-perfect-adic-space dictionary; Diamonds enlarges the target category to locally spatial diamonds (sheaves of perfectoid spaces), licensing limits and quotients KL couldn't form.

Pro-étale cohomology of perfectoid spaces vs. base-change-invariance of étale cohomology under extension of base fields — both rest on the same framework: pro-étale topology lifts naturally through perfectoid covers, with cohomology behaving stably.

Slope filtrations over the Robba ring (KL) and crystalline comparison in characteristic $p$ (prismatic) both organize $\varphi$-module data via 'slope = Newton-polygon-like invariant': KL gets a unique filtration by saturated submodules with strictly decreasing slopes; prismatic uses the perfect-prism crystalline identification to access the same slope structure.

Recovering classical $p$-adic Hodge / $(\varphi, \Gamma)$-module theory (KL) and constructing a Nygaard-filtered prismatic site (prismatic) both yield filtered cohomology theories whose graded pieces recover classical Galois-representation data. KL via Robba/period rings; prismatic via $\delta$-rings + quasisyntomic descent.

FS's geometric Satake construction works in a category whose objects include $B$-pairs and $\Gamma$-equivariant vector bundles on FF — exactly the categorical equivalences KL set up at thm:9.5.8. KL's framework supplies the categorical input for FS's dual-group construction.

$\varphi^a$-modules over the relative Robba ring (KL) and $A_{\mathrm{inf}}$-perfect complexes with $\varphi$ (BMS1) are independent realizations of 'perfect $\varphi$-equivariant data over a Witt-style ring'. Together they comprise the algebraic backbone the prismatic framework later abstracts.

Geometric variation of slopes (KL) and concentration in degree zero of derived prismatic cohomology over large quasisyntomic algebras (prismatic) both rest on flatness/degeneration arguments that propagate point-wise structure to the family. KL: slope polygon semicontinuity; prismatic: $(p,I)$-completely flat $\delta$-algebra structure.

$\mathrm{Bun}_G$ as a $v$-stack on $\mathrm{Perf}$ inherits its étale-topos behavior from the étale-site tilting Diamonds imports from Scholze. FS uses this implicitly throughout when manipulating $\ell$-adic sheaves on $\mathrm{Bun}_G$.

BMS1's perfectness of $R\Gamma_{A_{\mathrm{inf}}}(\mathfrak{X})$ becomes a special case of prismatic cohomology over the perfect prism $(A_{\mathrm{inf}}, \ker\theta)$. Prismatic's master comparison thm:1.8 generalizes the BMS1 perfectness statement to arbitrary bounded prisms.

BMS1's $B_{\mathrm{dR}}^+$-cohomology comparison is upgraded by prismatic's étale comparison theorem: nearby-cycles cohomology on the adic generic fibre identified as Frobenius-fixed inverted-$d$ prismatic cohomology. BMS1 is the rational/de Rham case; prismatic supplies the integral version.

BMS2 identifies $\widehat{\mathbbl{\Delta}}_A \simeq A\Omega_A$; prismatic's thm:13.1 sharpens this by giving the Nygaard completion a $\delta$-ring structure refining $\varphi$. The bridge BMS2 ↔ BMS1 ↔ prismatic is now triangulated.

Diamonds' imported almost-purity (Scholze, Kedlaya–Liu) and prismatic almost-purity (Bhatt–Scholze via André's flatness) are different formulations / reach of the same theorem the perfectoid program keeps strengthening. The shared tool is 'finite étale cover stays inside the perfectoid world up to almost-isomorphism'.

Bhatt–Lurie open by quoting BMS1's master $A\Omega$ comparison verbatim — the relative-perfectoid theorem the absolute version generalizes. The import is explicit (BMS1 thm:1.10 cited in the statement of thm:1.2.1).
BMS2's QRS-perfectoid $\mathrm{TC}^- \cong A_{\mathrm{inf}}$ identification imported verbatim as input to the Chern-class machinery.
BMS2 motivic filtration on $\mathrm{TP}$ imported and lifted to absolute prismatic setting via syntomic-site colimit description.

Bhatt–Lurie's absolute char-$p$ prismatic = crystalline extends Bhatt–Scholze's relative version (which lives over a fixed prism $(A,(p))$): the absolute case identifies $R\Gamma_{\mathbbl{\Delta}}(X)$ on the absolute prismatic site with $R\Gamma_{\mathrm{crys}}(X/\mathbb{Z}_p)$ for any quasisyntomic $\mathbb{F}_p$-scheme.
Étale-syntomic comparison extends Bhatt–Scholze's prismatic étale comparison to the absolute setting with explicit fibre description.

The Cartier-Witt stack's Hodge-Tate locus $\mathrm{WCart}^{\mathrm{HT}} \cong \mathrm{Spf}(\mathbb{Z}_p)$ is the *geometric* incarnation of Bhatt–Scholze's perfect-prism dictionary — the algebraic equivalence becomes an isomorphism of stacks. Same content, geometrically reformulated.
Combined Chern-class isomorphism unifies BMS1's $A\Omega$ comparison and the synthetic / de Rham / crystalline Chern classes into one universal coherent system over the absolute prismatic site.

Drinfeld's prismatization stack $\Sigma'(S) = \{(M, \xi) : M \text{ admissible }W_S\text{-module}, \xi: M \to W_S \text{ primitive}\}$ is a moduli-theoretic restatement of Bhatt–Scholze's perfect-prism dictionary $(A,I)$. Same data — admissible Witt-vector module + primitive morphism — geometrically packaged as a stack.

Drinfeld's algebraicity / Mor-affine / formal-c-stack structure on $\Sigma'$ is an alternative geometric engine for prismatic cohomology: where Bhatt–Scholze define the prismatic site and prove the master theorem object-wise, Drinfeld presents the underlying moduli stack and derives properties stack-theoretically.

Drinfeld's $\Sigma'$ and Bhatt–Lurie's Cartier-Witt stack $\mathrm{WCart}$ are independent moduli-theoretic incarnations of absolute prismatic geometry. Parallel reformulations (~2020 vs 2022); both algebraic stacks classifying admissible Witt-vector modules + primitive morphism, with different presentation.

Drinfeld's rigid fibre $\Sigma'_{\mathrm{Rig}}$ and the Fargues–Fontaine curve are both 'rigid-analytic shadows' of perfectoid moduli. Different specialisations: $\Sigma'_{\mathrm{Rig}}$ classifies admissible-module data; FF curve classifies vector bundles with prescribed slopes.
Anschütz–Le Bras explicitly imports Bhatt–Scholze's Hodge-Tate comparison (thm:6.3 in prismatic, cited as 'Theorem 4.10' in their numbering) as the structural backbone for prismatic Dieudonné theory.
ALB explicitly imports Bhatt–Scholze's char-$p$ crystalline comparison as the input that makes prismatic Dieudonné modules track crystalline Dieudonné modules in characteristic $p$.
ALB explicitly imports Bhatt–Scholze's André flatness lemma as the absolutely-integrally-closed-cover input for QRS-perfectoid descent.

ALB's identification of prismatic Dieudonné with Breuil–Kisin minimal modules over regular local rings extends Fargues's BKF module classification (BMS1 thm:4.28). BMS1 classifies finite-free BKF modules over $A_{\mathrm{inf}}$ for algebraically closed $C$; ALB extends to general regular local rings.

ALB's antiequivalence between $p$-divisible groups and admissible prismatic Dieudonné modules over quasi-syntomic $R$ extends Scholze–Weinstein's Hodge-Tate classification of $p$-divisible groups over $\mathcal{O}_C$ (which equates them with pairs $(T, W)$ of free $\mathbb{Z}_p$-module + $C$-subspace). ALB lifts the classification beyond $\mathcal{O}_C$ to all quasi-syntomic rings.

(Numbering coincidence: both papers' theorem 5.1.4.) ALB's exact antiequivalence on finite group schemes extends SW's quasi-isogeny lifting result for $p$-divisible groups — same Dieudonné-style classification, broader category, prismatic engine.

Algebraic-side companion to SW thm:6.3.4 ↔ BMS1 thm:4.28 already in the graph: SW's Dieudonné lifting and BMS1's BKF module classification are sister statements organizing the same $p$-divisible-group data — SW from the moduli side, BMS1 from the linear-algebra side.

SW's cartesian diagram for $\mathcal{M}_\infty$ (Hodge-Tate periods + universal cover description) provides the geometric input for FS's FF-curve classification: the period-map structure SW set up is what makes the Newton stratification + isocrystal classification on $\mathrm{Bun}_G$ tractable.

SW's preperfectoid $\mathcal{M}_\infty$ (infinite-level Rapoport–Zink space, $\sim \varprojlim \mathcal{M}_n$) and KL's perfectoid adic-space framework are both organizing 'limits of adic spaces become perfectoid' structures; SW provides the application-side example, KL provides the systematic categorical framework.
